text: Robotic revision of hepatico-gigenostomy. We present here a patient who had a history of chronic pancreatitis and benign biliary stricture. The patient underwent numerous endoscopic procedures and ERCPs approximately ten years ago, but ultimately required a surgical biliary bypass procedure. This was done in an outside facility in two thousand and nine and the patient did well for several years.

In the past several months the patient began developing recurring episodes of cholangitis. Imaging revealed multiple intrahepatic stones and biliary dilatation consistent with a stricture at the previous choledochogigenostomy.

The surgeon uses robotic assistance to perform a minimally invasive revision of the anastomosis, resecting the biliary stricture and creating a new hepaticogigenostomy. Adhesions from the previous open procedure are carefully dissected, and the roux limb of the small intestines is exposed in the right upper quadrant.

The surgeon uses robotic monopolar scissors to amputate the old anastomosis site. Visible vegetable and other debris are present within the limb. The distal aspect of the bile duct is dissected free from surrounding adhesions.

The surgeon uses robotic scissors to carefully dissect around structures in the porta hepatis, entering the dilated segment of the bile duct through the stricture. Approximately two centimeters of the bile duct are amputated, and the old cystic artery is identified and clipped, facilitating the removal of the strictured bile duct.

The surgeon mobilizes the existing Roux limb of the small intestine in the right upper quadrant, aiming to maximize its length for the procedure without reconstructing the entire limb. The bile duct margin is sent for frozen section analysis to rule out malignancy.
The surgeon closes the old anastomosis site using a running absorbable barbed suture, applying it in a single layer to secure the closure.

The surgeon pexies the roux limb to the anterior wall of the stomach to prevent retraction. Using monopolar scissors, a new enterotomy is created for the anastomosis, approximately one centimeter in diameter. The new anastomosis is initiated with a running absorbable monofilament barbed suture, starting at the nine o'clock position on the posterior wall and running medially towards the three o'clock position.

The surgeon uses a robotic platform to initiate a suture at the nine o'clock position, running it in a forehand fashion along the anterior wall of the anastomosis. Blue cones indicate the trainee performing portions of the procedure under faculty supervision.
The surgeon ties together the two cut ends of the absorbable suture, completing the revisional hepaticojejunostomy anastomosis.
We elected, obviously, to leave a drain as usual, although that was removed.
